spec_match.rationale: Honest framing: 'the replacement for XSLT isn't a polyfill — it's a JavaScript transform'. Demonstrates three approaches (native XSLT for contrast, XPath via document.evaluate, DOM walk) against the same XML.

interactive.rationale: Three sample inputs (catalog/rss/atom), three render strategies, code panel + output panel. 5 controls.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Index promises 'same XML rendered three ways: native XSLT (probed for support), an XPath-based JS transform, and a DOM walk' — page delivers.

edge_cases.rationale: The XSLT renderer throws by design (the comment says 'XSLT 1.0 — browser support removed in Chrome 143'). Doesn't actually attempt the XSLT path on browsers that still expose XSLTProcessor — so the 'probed for support' claim in the blurb isn't quite right. The page treats XSLT as already-removed regardless of the browser.

references.rationale: See-also includes https://chromestatus.com/feature/4709671889534976 and the WHATWG removal issue.

design_system.rationale: Variables-only; code panels use --bg-stone with mono font.

openQuestions.title: Actually attempt the XSLT path on browsers that still expose it
openQuestions.detail: The xslt renderer is a stub that throws — but Chrome ≤142 and Firefox still have XSLTProcessor. The blurb says 'probed for support', so the page should: if XSLTProcessor exists, run a real transform; otherwise show the removal notice. As-is the page hides the contrast it claims to show.

openQuestions.title: Add an XSL stylesheet that turns into each JS renderer
openQuestions.detail: Showing the actual XSL the legacy approach would have used (with `<xsl:template match='product'>` etc.) and putting it side-by-side with each JS renderer would teach migrators how to translate XSL templates into JS. That's the most valuable angle for sites trying to migrate.

openQuestions.title: Add a Saxon-JS or fontoxpath link
openQuestions.detail: If a site really needs XSLT 2.0/3.0, Saxon-JS exists. Even a one-line note in the page pointing migrators to that option (and explaining the bundle-size cost) would help the audience that *can't* just rewrite as JS.

openQuestions.title: Show the input XML and the XPath at the same time
openQuestions.detail: When users click 'xpath', they see the JS but not the XPath highlighted against the input. A dual-pane view (left: XML with the matched nodes highlighted, right: the XPath expression) would make the XPath approach pedagogically stronger.

summary: Strongest concept in this feature for migration audiences — three approaches on the same data. Big fix: actually attempt the XSLT path on browsers that still expose it (the blurb says 'probed for support' but the stub always throws), and add the XSL stylesheet alongside the JS renderers so migrators can map old → new.
